{"covered_dims": {"R1.7": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.4, "R1.4": 0.2}}

Citation audit of the NumClarify section. Summary: the three explicit citations are real works correctly attributed, but two of the three are weak/dated choices for the load-bearing claims they support, and roughly a half-dozen substantive claims in the section name specific methods and research programs without citing anything.

**Explicit citations — verification**

1. Hageman & Young (1981), *Applied Iterative Methods*, Academic Press. Real book; correctly attributed; does present iterative methods via matrix splittings (Jacobi/Gauss–Seidel/SOR/SSOR and block variants). But the text leans on it as "the most comprehensive prior treatment of iterative-solver taxonomy that we adopt as our foundational organizing reference," which is a stretch:
   - It predates the Krylov-subspace literature you actually dispatch into (CG is older but its modern presentation, MINRES, GMRES, BiCGStab, IDR, etc. are post-1981 or only nascent there).
   - The natural foundational reference for the taxonomy you describe (SPD → CG, symmetric indefinite → MINRES, nonsymmetric → GMRES, plus preconditioning families) is Saad's *Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems* (2nd ed., 2003) — which you already cite at the end for preconditioners — or Greenbaum, *Iterative Methods for Solving Linear Systems* (SIAM, 1997), or Barrett et al., *Templates for the Solution of Linear Systems* (SIAM, 1994). Recommend either (a) replace Hageman & Young with one of these as the organizing reference and retain H&Y only where splitting-based stationary methods specifically appear, or (b) keep H&Y but narrow the claim to "splitting-based stationary iterations."

2. Anderson et al. (1992), *LAPACK Users' Guide*, 1st ed., SIAM. Real; correct authorship (Anderson, Bai, Bischof, Demmel, Dongarra, Du Croz, Greenbaum, Hammarling, McKenney, Ostrouchov, Sorensen). `?gesv` and `?posv` are genuine LAPACK driver routine names and the mapping you describe is reasonable. However, citing the 1st edition is unusual — the 3rd edition (Anderson et al., 1999, SIAM, ISBN 0-89871-447-8) is the standard reference and is what most readers will expect; the driver-routine catalogue is also more complete there. Recommend switching to the 3rd edition unless you specifically need a 1992-era statement.

3. Saad (2003), *Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems*, 2nd ed., SIAM. Real; appropriate for preconditioner-family selection. No issue.

**Substantive claims with missing citations**

- "randomized numerical linear algebra" — should cite Halko, Martinsson & Tropp, *SIAM Review* 53(2), 2011 ("Finding Structure with Randomness"), and/or Martinsson & Tropp, *Acta Numerica* 29, 2020 ("Randomized numerical linear algebra: Foundations and algorithms").
- "communication-avoiding Krylov methods" — should cite Hoemmen, PhD thesis, UC Berkeley, 2010 ("Communication-Avoiding Krylov Subspace Methods"), and/or Carson, Knight & Demmel, *SIAM J. Sci. Comput.* 35(5), 2013.
- "mixed-precision iterative refinement on accelerator hardware" / "GMRES-IR family … recently formalized for ill-conditioned systems on modern GPUs" — should cite Carson & Higham, *SIAM J. Sci. Comput.* 39(6), 2017 and 40(2), 2018 (the GMRES-IR papers), and the GPU realization by Haidar, Tomov, Dongarra & Higham, *SC'18* ("Harnessing GPU Tensor Cores for Fast FP16 Arithmetic to Speed up Mixed-Precision Iterative Refinement Solvers").
- "tile-based block layout … task-parallel runtimes such as PLASMA and SLATE" — PLASMA: Agullo, Demmel, Dongarra et al., *J. Phys.: Conf. Ser.* 180, 2009, or Buttari, Langou, Kurzak & Dongarra, *Parallel Computing* 35(1), 2009. SLATE: Gates, Kurzak, Charara, YarKhan & Dongarra, *SC'19* ("SLATE: Design of a Modern Distributed and Accelerated Linear Algebra Library"). At minimum cite one canonical paper per package.
- "CG, MINRES, or restarted GMRES" — the methods themselves warrant the standard primary references at first mention: Hestenes & Stiefel, *J. Res. Nat. Bur. Stand.* 49, 1952 (CG); Paige & Saunders, *SIAM J. Numer. Anal.* 12(4), 1975 (MINRES); Saad & Schultz, *SIAM J. Sci. Stat. Comput.* 7(3), 1986 (GMRES).
- "standard backward-error analysis" — for the residual / backward-error / forward-error distinction underlying the tolerance probe, cite Higham, *Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms*, 2nd ed., SIAM, 2002 (Chs. 1–2, 7).
- The LAPACK condition-number-aware dispatch (`xGESVX`, `xPOSVX` expert drivers, `xGECON`) merits a pointer rather than only the base drivers — same Anderson et al. citation, but signal which driver class supports the conditioning estimate.

**Internal inconsistency**

Saad (2003) is cited only for preconditioners in the final paragraph but is the obvious source for the symmetry-based dispatch taxonomy two paragraphs earlier. Either cite Saad at the dispatch claim too, or be explicit that the dispatch taxonomy is from Hageman & Young while preconditioning is from Saad — currently the reader has to infer this.

**Uncertainty disclosure**

I have not opened the cited books to verify exact pagination of the `?gesv`/`?posv` driver descriptions in the 1992 edition specifically (vs. the 1995/1999 editions); the routines exist in all editions but coverage depth differs. The Carson–Higham GMRES-IR sequence has additional follow-up papers (2020, 2022); I cited the foundational two but a current survey may now be preferable. The PLASMA citation I suggested is one of several plausible canonical references — Dongarra's group has published multiple overview papers and you may have a preferred one.
